blocks.text: Chapter 6.
The Morality of Manipulation

Put the tools down for a moment, friend, and look at what we've built across these nights. A trigger that finds you in your weakest feeling. An action shaved down to a single effortless tap. A reward that flickers like a slot machine. An investment that locks the door behind you, gently, with your own hands.

It works. That is no longer in question. It works on adults, on children, on the lonely, on the bored — on the very feelings people most wish someone would not exploit. So tonight the storyteller stops narrating and asks the question the whole book has been walking toward:
Just because you can... should you?
The uncomfortable word

Eyal does not flinch from the word: what this book teaches is manipulation — deliberately designing experiences to change other people's behavior. And he refuses both easy exits. He won't pretend it's innocent: these techniques are the same compulsion machinery casinos run, and "we're just giving users what they want" is the oldest sentence in the dealer's dictionary. But he also won't pretend it's all evil: a toothbrushing app, a language streak, a running habit — the identical hook can install those too. The machinery is amoral. The builder is not. So the weight lands where it belongs: on the person holding the tools — which, after six chapters, includes you.
What Eyal offers is not absolution but a mirror — a simple instrument he calls the Manipulation Matrix, built from just two questions.
Pause and guess: Two questions, answered honestly, sort every builder of habit-forming products into one of four moral positions. Before I reveal them — what two questions would you put on that test?
Ready? Here is what happened.

Eyal's two: "Would I use this product myself?" and "Does it materially improve the user's life?" Notice how cunning the pairing is. The first question outlaws the oldest excuse in tech — it's fine for other people — because if you won't swallow your own medicine, you already know something your marketing doesn't say. The second draws the line between real value and mere engagement: "time spent in app" improves the company's life, not the user's. Cross the two answers and four characters appear — the facilitator, the peddler, the entertainer, and the dealer. Every product you've ever loved or resented was built by one of them.
The four builders

The Facilitator — uses it, and it improves lives. The maker who builds something they genuinely need and would hand to their own family. They understand the itch from inside, so the habit they install serves the user first. This is the strongest ground to build from, morally and commercially — authentic need is the best product insight there is.

The Peddler — doesn't use it, but insists it improves lives. The fitness app built by someone with no interest in fitness; the education startup founded on a market gap, not a lived need. Not villains — often idealists. But Eyal's warning is sharp: peddlers usually fail, because they are designing for an itch they have never felt, guessing at feelings from the outside. Good intentions are not user insight — and the graveyard of do-gooder apps proves it.

The Entertainer — uses it, but can't claim it improves lives. The game maker, the meme app, the pleasant distraction. Eyal is gentle here: entertainment is ancient and honorable — art doesn't have to be medicine. But its habits are fads that burn bright and fade; the entertainer must keep shipping the next hit, forever chasing what the facilitator builds once.

The Dealer — wouldn't use it, and it improves nothing. Exploitation, plain and simple: the casino logic wearing an app icon, extracting hours and money from people the builder privately pities. Eyal's judgment is flat — this ground is morally indefensible, and history adds its own verdict: dealers end up regulated, sued, or despised, because a business built on its users' weakness is a business waiting for a reckoning.
The mirror turned around
Pause and guess: Now the storyteller's twist. We've judged the builders — but you hold this same matrix from the other side of the glass. How does it work as a user's tool?
Ready? Here is what happened.

Point the two questions at every app on your phone — reversed: "Would the people who built this let their own children use it the way I use it?" and "Is this materially improving my life — or just my screen time?" Tech executives famously restricting their own kids' access to the very products they ship is the peddler's confession, spoken in parenting. Sort your home screen into the four quadrants, honestly: the facilitators you keep gladly, the entertainers you keep knowingly and in moderation, the peddlers you forgive or drop... and the dealers you delete tonight. There is real power in this little audit: the machinery of the hook only owns people who haven't seen it — and you, friend, have now seen all four gears and the hands that turn them.
Reading it from both sides of the glass

For the builder, the matrix is not a wall plaque — it is a gate you must actually stand at. Answer the two questions before the roadmap, not after the funding round. If you land in the facilitator's square, build with a clear conscience — habits that serve users are a gift, and this book is your toolbox. If you land anywhere else, at least know where you stand — self-deception, not manipulation, is where the real rot starts.

For the human: mercy for yourself, too. You were never weak for being hooked — you were outnumbered; thousands of engineers, one of you, and the machinery aimed at feelings you didn't choose to have. But knowledge redistributes the power. You know the four gears now. You know the four builders. The question that remains is only ever the next small act: which app, tonight, goes in which square.
Before you go

One night remains in this story, and it belongs to the practical question every builder in the room is already asking: how do I actually find my hook? How do you discover which of your users are habits waiting to happen, what they're doing differently, and how to invite everyone else down the same path?
Eyal's answer is a method — Habit Testing — plus a treasure map: where new habit opportunities hide, and why the next great hook is probably sitting in a behavior you already do without noticing it.
Last time, next time: Habit Testing, and where to look.
Key takeaways
The hook is manipulation — deliberately amoral machinery; the morality lives entirely in the builder.
Two questions sort every builder: Would I use it myself? Does it materially improve users' lives?
Facilitators build from lived need and stand strongest; peddlers guess at itches they've never felt — and usually fail.
Entertainers are honorable but ephemeral; dealers run casino logic and inherit the casino's reckoning.
Turn the matrix on your own phone: keep the facilitators, ration the entertainers, delete the dealers tonight.
You were never weak — you were outnumbered. Seeing the gears is what evens the odds.
